title: llama.cpp
lesson:
llama.cpp is a small, fast program that runs a language model on your own machine — your laptop, a Mac, a single graphics card, even a Raspberry Pi. It is written in C++ (a low-level programming language that compiles down to fast machine code), which is where the .cpp comes from.

The whole point: take a big model and make it answer prompts on ordinary hardware, with no Python, no cloud account, and no data-center GPU required. It is the engine underneath many friendlier tools you may have heard of, including Ollama and LM Studio.

The car-engine analogy

Think of running a model locally like driving a car. Most people want the dashboard, the seats, and a key to turn — that is what Ollama gives you. But under the hood sits an engine doing the real work. llama.cpp is that engine.

It is lean, it has very few dependencies (extra software it needs to run), and it is built to squeeze every drop of speed out of the metal it runs on. You can drive the bare engine directly with command-line tools, and many builders do, because it gives total control. Others bolt a nicer body on top.

Why it exists: do the math where you are

A language model is, at heart, a giant pile of numbers (its parameters) and a lot of multiplication. The original tools assumed you would do that multiplication on an expensive GPU using PyTorch (a heavy Python framework for machine learning). llama.cpp threw out that assumption.

It does the math directly in optimized C++ and can run on:

  • a plain CPU,
  • Apple Silicon (the chips in modern Macs),
  • or a GPU.

Better still, it can split one model across your GPU and ordinary computer memory at the same time. So a model too big for your graphics card still runs; it just leans on system memory for the overflow and goes a bit slower.

GGUF: the fuel it burns

llama.cpp reads models in one specific format: GGUF, a single self-contained file that bundles the weights, tokenizer, and chat template together. GGUF has quantization baked in — storing each number with fewer bits to save space, like vacuum-packing clothes so the same items fill a quarter of the suitcase.

This is the second reason llama.cpp can run on modest hardware: an 8-billion-parameter model that needs roughly 16 GB at full precision shrinks to roughly 5 GB at the popular 4-bit setting, small enough to fit on a normal laptop.

Using it directly

You download or build the program, point it at a .gguf file, and talk to it. Two common ways: one-shot from the command line, or as a small server that speaks the same API shape as OpenAI's (so existing code can talk to it unchanged).

# One-shot: ask a single question
./llama-cli -m llama-3.1-8b-instruct.Q4_K_M.gguf \
  -p "Explain a black hole to a 10-year-old." -n 200

# Or run it as a local server on port 8080
./llama-server -m llama-3.1-8b-instruct.Q4_K_M.gguf \
  --ctx-size 4096 --n-gpu-layers 35

Once the server is running, your app calls it like any chat API — just pointed at your own computer instead of the cloud:

curl http://localhost:8080/v1/chat/completions \
  -d '{"messages":[{"role":"user","content":"Hello!"}]}'

That --n-gpu-layers flag is the knob that matters most. A model is built in stacked layers; this number says how many of them to push onto the GPU. More layers on the GPU means faster answers — up to the point where your GPU memory (VRAM) is full. Set it too high and llama.cpp runs out of room and crashes; the trick is to raise it until it just fits.

Where it fits — and where it doesn't

llama.cpp shines for local inference and on-device AI: one user, one machine, privacy kept at home, no per-token bill. It is the wrong tool for serving thousands of users at once — for that, a GPU-server engine like vLLM is built to handle many requests in parallel.

Think of llama.cpp as a brilliant home espresso machine and vLLM as the industrial setup behind a busy café counter.

💡 If you just want a model running in two minutes, use Ollama (it bundles llama.cpp). Reach for raw llama.cpp when you need fine control over speed, memory, and exactly which build runs.
takeaways: llama.cpp is the lean C++ engine that runs LLMs locally — no Python, no cloud.
It reads one quantized .gguf file and can split a model across GPU and RAM.
Great for one user on one machine; use vLLM to serve a crowd.

diagram.svg: <svg class="dg" viewBox="0 0 720 340" role="img" aria-label="How llama.cpp runs a GGUF model locally and splits it across GPU and system memory"><defs><marker id="arrow" markerWidth="12" markerHeight="12" refX="9" refY="5" orient="auto"><path class="dg-arrowhead" d="M0,0 L11,5 L0,10 z"/></marker></defs><text class="dg-title" x="360" y="34" text-anchor="middle">One GGUF file, split across your hardware</text><rect class="dg-box dg-box-accent" x="40" y="130" width="150" height="70" rx="12"/><text class="dg-label" x="115" y="160" text-anchor="middle">model.gguf</text><text class="dg-sub" x="115" y="182" text-anchor="middle">weights + tokenizer</text><rect class="dg-box" x="265" y="130" width="170" height="70" rx="12"/><text class="dg-label" x="350" y="160" text-anchor="middle">llama.cpp</text><text class="dg-sub" x="350" y="182" text-anchor="middle">C++ engine</text><line class="dg-edge" x1="190" y1="165" x2="258" y2="165" marker-end="url(#arrow)"/><rect class="dg-box dg-box-good" x="515" y="70" width="165" height="60" rx="12"/><text class="dg-label" x="597" y="95" text-anchor="middle">GPU (VRAM)</text><text class="dg-sub" x="597" y="115" text-anchor="middle">--n-gpu-layers</text><rect class="dg-box dg-box-warn" x="515" y="200" width="165" height="60" rx="12"/><text class="dg-label" x="597" y="225" text-anchor="middle">System RAM</text><text class="dg-sub" x="597" y="245" text-anchor="middle">overflow layers</text><line class="dg-edge" x1="435" y1="150" x2="512" y2="105" marker-end="url(#arrow)"/><line class="dg-edge" x1="435" y1="180" x2="512" y2="225" marker-end="url(#arrow)"/><text class="dg-note" x="360" y="308" text-anchor="middle">More layers on the GPU = faster, until VRAM is full; the rest spills to RAM.</text></svg>
diagram.caption: llama.cpp loads a single GGUF file and offloads as many layers as fit onto the GPU, leaving the overflow in system RAM.

quiz.q: Which file format does llama.cpp load directly to run a model?
options: A Hugging Face folder of .safetensors files
quiz.explain: llama.cpp reads GGUF — one self-contained file with weights, tokenizer, and chat template. Other formats (like safetensors) must be converted to .gguf first.

quiz.q: You raise --n-gpu-layers higher and higher. What happens when you go past what your VRAM can hold?
options: It runs out of GPU memory and fails to load (or crashes)
It quietly switches the model to a smaller one
quiz.explain: Each layer pushed to the GPU needs VRAM. Once you exceed it, llama.cpp runs out of room — the fix is to raise the number gradually until it just fits, then stop.

quiz.q: A startup needs to serve a chat model to thousands of simultaneous users with high throughput. Why is raw llama.cpp usually the wrong choice here?
options: It cannot run on a GPU at all
It is tuned for one or a few users, not heavy parallel batching like vLLM
It only works on Apple Silicon
It cannot expose an HTTP API
quiz.explain: llama.cpp shines for single-user local inference; for many concurrent requests you want a batching engine such as vLLM. (llama.cpp can use a GPU and does expose an OpenAI-style server, so those options are false.)

quiz.q: An 8B model is too big for your 6 GB graphics card. How can llama.cpp still run it on your laptop?
options: It refuses to load and tells you to buy a bigger GPU
It splits the model — some layers on the GPU, the overflow in system RAM
It retrains the model to be smaller on the fly
It streams the weights from the internet each token
quiz.explain: llama.cpp can offload only the layers that fit into VRAM and keep the rest in ordinary system memory. The model runs — just slower for the part living in RAM.

mentalModel: llama.cpp is the lean C++ engine under the hood of local AI: feed it one quantized GGUF file and it runs the model fast on a plain laptop, Mac, or single GPU — no Python, no cloud. Friendly tools like Ollama are just a nicer car body wrapped around this same engine.
mistakes: Feeding it the wrong file format. llama.cpp only runs GGUF files. Pointing it at a Hugging Face safetensors folder will not work — you must convert to .gguf first (llama.cpp ships a converter script).

Maxing out --n-gpu-layers blindly. Pushing every layer onto the GPU is fastest only if it fits. Past your VRAM limit it crashes or silently slows down. Raise the number gradually until it just fits, then stop.

Expecting it to serve a crowd. llama.cpp is tuned for one or a few users on one machine. For many simultaneous requests you want a batching engine like vLLM; do not try to scale llama.cpp into a busy production API for thousands.

Setting the context size larger than your memory allows. A bigger --ctx-size (how many tokens the model can hold at once) eats memory fast because of the KV cache. Asking for a huge context on a small machine can fail to load or grind to a halt.

Picking too aggressive a quantization to save space. Tiny quants like Q2_K shrink the file but visibly hurt answers. Start at Q4_K_M; only go lower if the model genuinely will not fit. The format is GGUF; the quality knob is the quantization tag inside it.

Using a stale build with a newer model. llama.cpp moves fast and new model architectures need recent code. If a brand-new model fails to load with a cryptic error, rebuild llama.cpp from the latest source before assuming the model file is broken.
exercise:
Get llama.cpp running on your own machine. Download a small instruct model in GGUF (a 1B–3B model at Q4_K_M is ideal), build or install llama.cpp, and use llama-cli to ask it one question from the command line. Then start it with llama-server and send the same question with a curl request to localhost. Note how many tokens per second it reports — that is your local speed.

Stretch goal: Re-run the same model while changing --n-gpu-layers from 0 (CPU only) up toward the full layer count, recording tokens-per-second at each step. Find the highest value that still loads without running out of memory, and watch where the speed stops improving.
solution: Hint: The easiest install path is your package manager (for example brew install llama.cpp on a Mac) or downloading a prebuilt release; building from source with cmake is also fine if you want the very latest. For the curl step, the server defaults to port 8080 and mimics the OpenAI chat API, so hit http://localhost:8080/v1/chat/completions. For the stretch goal, expect a clear pattern: at 0 layers (pure CPU) it is slowest; speed climbs as you move layers onto the GPU; once the whole model fits in VRAM, adding more does nothing because there is nothing left to move. If you set the number too high it will refuse to load (out of memory) — back it off by a few layers.
summary: llama.cpp is a lean C++ engine that runs language models locally on CPUs, Macs, and single GPUs, with no Python or cloud needed. It reads quantized GGUF files and can split a model across GPU and system memory, so even large models run on modest hardware. It powers friendly wrappers like Ollama and is perfect for single-user local inference — but for serving many users at once, reach for a batching engine like vLLM instead.

keyTerms.def: A fast, dependency-light C++ program that runs language models locally on CPUs, Apple Silicon, and GPUs. The engine underneath tools like Ollama and LM Studio.
The single-file model format llama.cpp reads. It bundles weights, tokenizer, and chat template together, with quantization built in for easy local running.
Storing each weight with fewer bits to shrink the model, like vacuum-packing it. A 4-bit setting can cut an 8B model from roughly 16 GB down to roughly 5 GB.
The llama.cpp setting for how many of a model's stacked layers to run on the GPU. Higher means faster, until you run out of GPU memory.
Splitting one model between the GPU and ordinary system memory so a model too big for the graphics card still runs — just slower for the part on system memory.
llama.cpp's built-in web server. It exposes an OpenAI-compatible chat API on your own machine, so existing app code can talk to a local model unchanged.
How many tokens the model can hold at once (the --ctx-size flag). Larger contexts use more memory because the KV cache grows with them.
The chips in modern Macs (M-series). llama.cpp is heavily optimized for them, using the GPU and unified memory to run models fast without a separate graphics card.
Extra software a program needs to run. llama.cpp deliberately has very few, which is why it installs and runs cleanly on almost any machine.
